questions.question: What is the measure of a full turn in degrees?
questions.explanation: A full turn has been divided into 360°.

questions.question: What is the reason for using 360° to divide a circle?
questions.correct_answer: The reason is not fully known.
questions.explanation: The division of a circle into 360° was chosen, but the exact reason is unknown.

questions.question: How many degrees are in a straight angle?
questions.explanation: A straight angle is half of a full turn, so it measures 180°.

questions.question: What is the measure of a right angle in degrees?
questions.explanation: Two right angles together form a straight angle, which measures 180°. So, one right angle measures 90°.

questions.question: How many degrees are in an acute angle?
questions.explanation: An acute angle is less than a right angle, so it is less than 90°.

questions.question: What is the difference between an acute and obtuse angle?
questions.correct_answer: Acute angles have smaller openings, while obtuse angles have larger openings.
questions.explanation: The terms 'acute' and 'obtuse' refer to the size of the opening in the angle.

questions.question: How many acute angles are there in a triangle?
questions.explanation: In any triangle, three angles are always acute.

questions.question: What is the measure of an angle that is half of a full turn?
questions.explanation: A straight angle is half of a full turn, so it measures 180°.

questions.question: How many right angles are in a straight angle?
questions.explanation: Two right angles together form a straight angle.

questions.question: What is the measure of an obtuse angle?
questions.explanation: An obtuse angle is greater than a right angle, but less than a straight angle.

important_notes.title: Key Concept: Angle Measure
important_notes.content: The measure of an angle is the number of 1° unit parts it contains inside it.

important_notes.title: Important Note: Straight Angle
important_notes.content: A straight angle is half of a full turn and measures 180°.

important_notes.title: Key Concept: Acute Angle
important_notes.content: An acute angle has a smaller opening than a right angle and is less than 90°.

important_notes.title: Important Note: Obtuse Angle
important_notes.content: An obtuse angle has a larger opening than a right angle but is less than a straight angle.

important_notes.title: Key Concept: Right Angle
important_notes.content: A right angle measures 90° and is half of a straight angle.

important_notes.title: Important Note: Full Turn
important_notes.content: A full turn contains 360° and is used as the reference for measuring other angles.

important_notes.title: Key Concept: Degrees
important_notes.content: Degrees are used to measure angles, with 1 degree being equal to one unit part of a circle.

important_notes.title: Important Note: Unit Parts
important_notes.content: The number of 1° unit parts in an angle determines its measure.

important_notes.title: Key Concept: Angle Comparison
important_notes.content: Angles can be compared by superimposition, where the vertices and arms are aligned.

important_notes.title: Important Note: Equal Angles
important_notes.content: Two angles are equal if their vertices and arms overlap exactly.

facts.fact: The division of a circle into 360° is believed to have originated from the Babylonians.
The ancient Greeks used a sexagesimal (base-60) system for measuring angles, which led to the use of 360°.
In some cultures, a full turn is divided into 400° rather than 360°.
The concept of degrees as we know it today was not developed until the 17th century.
In ancient China, angles were measured using a system of rods and strings.
The Babylonians used a sexagesimal (base-60) system for measuring time as well as angles.
The use of 360° to divide a circle is not unique to Western cultures, but has been adopted by many cultures around the world.
In some mathematical systems, angles are measured in radians rather than degrees.
The concept of obtuse angles was not developed until the 16th century.
The ancient Greeks used a system of proportions to measure angles, which led to the development of trigonometry.
